content.problemStatement:
You are given a permutation PPP of integers from 111 to NNN, and an integer KKK such that ⌈2N3⌉≤K≤N\lceil \frac{2N}{3}\rceil \le K \le N⌈32N​⌉≤K≤N. In one operation, you can do the following:


Choose any consecutive segment of at most KKK elements of the permutation, and sort it in increasing order.
What's the smallest number of operations you have to make to sort the entire permutation in increasing order?


Input Format


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT  −-− the number of test cases. The description of test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains two integers N,KN, KN,K.




The second line of each test case contains NNN integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ −-− the elements of the permutation.





Output Format
For each test case, output the smallest number of operations you have to make to sort the entire permutation in increasing order.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K (⌈2N/3⌉ ≤ K ≤ N). The second line contains N distinct integers P1 … PN – the permutation of 1…N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum number of operations required to sort the whole permutation.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First case already sorted → 0. Second case the mismatched segment is positions 3‑4 (length 2 ≤ K) → 1. Third case mismatched segment is positions 1‑4 (length 4 > K) → needs 2 operations. Fourth case mismatched segment length is 5 > K, and because K=4 the minimum is 3 operations according to the optimal strategy described in the statement.
In the first test the only misplaced part is positions 1‑2 (length 2 ≤ K) → 1 operation. In the second test the whole array is reversed, the mismatched segment length is 5 > K, so the answer is 2.
Misplaced segment is the whole array (length 7 > K), therefore at least two operations are required.

hidden.label: edge case where segment length equals K

solution.approach: For each test case we first check whether the permutation is already sorted – answer 0. Otherwise we locate the leftmost index l where Pi ≠ i and the rightmost index r where Pi ≠ i. All misplaced elements lie inside the segment [l, r]. If the length of this segment (r‑l+1) does not exceed K we can sort this whole segment in one operation, so answer 1. If the segment is longer than K we cannot fix everything in a single operation, but because K ≥ ⌈2N/3⌉ it is always possible to sort the permutation in two operations (e.g., sort a prefix of length K that contains at least one correct border, then sort the remaining part). Hence the answer is 2 in this case. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over the whole input.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional besides the input array.
